rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe distress but haemodynamically stable with a reassuring red-flag history (no cauda equina features, normal urine). Telehealth cannot perform the spinal/neurological exam or CK needed to fully exclude the not-to-miss diagnoses, and the patient is isolated with overdose risk — so prompt in-person review (urgent, not emergency) is proportionate. T2 is the minimum viable pass. Escalate to T5 immediately if cauda equina or rhabdomyolysis features appear.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Loss of bladder or bowel control, saddle numbness, or sudden focal limb weakness
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate possible cauda equina syndrome — a surgical emergency where delay causes permanent damage.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your legs suddenly give way, you go numb around the saddle area, or you lose control of your bladder or bowels — call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Urine turns dark/tea-coloured or output drops markedly
escalation_edges.rationale: Dark urine suggests rhabdomyolysis with risk of acute kidney injury.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your urine turns dark like tea or you're passing very little, go to the Emergency Department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling unable to keep yourself safe or thoughts of taking too much medication
escalation_edges.rationale: Isolation, panic and stated overdose risk require timely support and safe management of medications.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel you might take too much medication or can't keep yourself safe, call for help now — a crisis line, a trusted person, or your after-hours service.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower threshold for arranging timely in-person support; check whether someone can attend and help manage medications safely.
Address the pain–panic cycle with calming measures and trauma-informed follow-up rather than sedatives.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This sounds like a severe flare of your post-injury pain rather than something newly broken, but you need an in-person examination soon. Keep your room quiet and dark, use slow breathing before moving, take no more anti-inflammatories today, and arrange to be seen within the next day or so.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Contact your GP/after-hours service for a prompt in-person review; use 000/ED for the emergency features below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If the emergency features appear after hours, use 000/ED rather than waiting.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note when this flare started, what you've already taken today, and that a neurological/spinal examination is needed.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Self-management advice without screening or safety-netting cauda equina/rhabdomyolysis in a patient who cannot be examined remotely.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving cauda equina is missed; the patient is left with permanent paralysis and incontinence.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Dispatching an ambulance for a stable central pain flare with a clean red-flag screen, medicalising a non-emergency.
